symbols: left panel garden with Adam and Eve — the original paradise and innocence before the Fall
central panel with nude multitudes — humanity given over to sensual pleasures and worldly indulgence
right panel of darkness and torment — hell and the consequences of sin
fantastical hybrid creatures — the corruption and distortion of God’s good creation
giant fruits — the allure and excess of earthly pleasure
musical instruments as torture devices — pleasures turned to punishment
fountain structures — parodies of the fountain of life
description: A large triptych with three distinct panels. The left panel shows a verdant garden with a robed figure presenting a woman to a man, surrounded by exotic animals and a fantastical pink fountain structure. The central panel teems with hundreds of nude human figures amid giant fruits, birds, pools, and surreal organic structures on a lush green landscape. The right panel is dark and nightmarish, filled with grotesque scenes of torment: mechanical devices, burning buildings, monstrous hybrid creatures, and suffering human figures in a hellish landscape.
theological_description: This triptych presents a sweeping theological narrative from Creation to Damnation. The left panel depicts the Garden of Eden with God presenting Eve to Adam (Genesis 2:22), establishing the original covenant of innocence. The central panel has been variously interpreted as humanity before the Flood given over to unrestrained sensuality, or as a moral allegory of the world’s seductive pleasures that lead souls astray. The abundance of flesh and fruit evokes the warning of 1 John 2:16: ‘the lust of the flesh, the lust of the eyes, and the pride of life.’ The right panel depicts a hellscape embodying the eschatological judgment described in Revelation 20:15 and Matthew 25:41, where those who pursued earthly delights face eternal torment. The triptych’s progression from paradise through indulgence to damnation mirrors the biblical arc of creation, fall, and judgment. It serves as a powerful visual sermon on the wages of sin (Romans 6:23) and the narrow gate that few find (Matthew 7:13-14).

scripture_refs.reason: The left panel depicts the Garden of Eden with the creation of Eve and the presentation to Adam
The Fall and its consequences are implied in the progression from innocence to indulgence to damnation
The central panel embodies the lust of the flesh, lust of the eyes, and pride of life
The right panel depicts the lake of fire and final judgment for those not found in the book of life
The broad way that leads to destruction, illustrated by the many figures pursuing earthly pleasures
The wages of sin is death, dramatized by the torments of the hellscape panel
